title: The digestive system — breaking food down
examTip: Digestive organ functions appear on virtually every Paper 1. You need each organ, its function, and its enzyme or secretion.
intro: Your digestive system is essentially a 9-metre tube that does one job: turn a meal into molecules small enough to enter your bloodstream. Every organ along the way has a specific role — and AQA will test you on all of them.

keyPoints.heading: Why digestion is necessary
keyPoints.content: Large food molecules like starch, protein and fat are insoluble — they can't pass through the gut wall into the blood. Digestion breaks them into small, soluble molecules that can be absorbed. Learn this definition word-for-word: digestion = the breakdown of large, insoluble food molecules into small, soluble molecules that can be absorbed into the blood.
writeBullets: Large food molecules are insoluble — cannot enter the blood
Digestion breaks them into small, soluble molecules
Definition: digestion = breakdown of large, insoluble molecules into small, soluble ones absorbed into the blood

keyTerms.def: The breakdown of large, insoluble food molecules into small, soluble molecules that can be absorbed into the blood
Cannot dissolve — too large to pass through the gut wall

keyPoints.heading: The route food takes
keyPoints.content: Food travels in order: mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → rectum. The liver, gall bladder and pancreas are not part of the main tube — they branch off and connect to the small intestine. Don't forget them.
writeBullets: Route: mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → rectum
Liver, gall bladder and pancreas branch off into the small intestine (not part of main tube)
Food moved by peristalsis — wave-like muscle contractions in the oesophagus

keyTerms.def: Muscular tube carrying food from the mouth to the stomach
Wave-like muscle contractions that push food along the digestive system

keyPoints.heading: Bile — the one that catches everyone out
keyPoints.content: Bile is NOT an enzyme. It's produced by the liver, stored in the gall bladder, and released into the small intestine. It emulsifies fats — breaks large fat droplets into smaller ones — giving lipase a larger surface area to work on. That's a physical change, not a chemical reaction. No catalysis = not an enzyme.
writeBullets: Bile is NOT an enzyme — it does not catalyse a chemical reaction
Bile made in the LIVER, stored in the GALL BLADDER
Emulsifies fats = breaks large fat droplets into smaller ones
This gives lipase a larger surface area to work on

keyTerms.def: A substance (not an enzyme) made in the liver that emulsifies fats in the small intestine
Breaking large fat droplets into smaller ones — a physical change, not chemical digestion
Organ that stores bile until it is needed in the small intestine

keyPoints.heading: Absorption — where digested food enters the blood
keyPoints.content: The small intestine is lined with millions of tiny finger-like projections called villi. These massively increase the surface area for absorption. Glucose and amino acids are absorbed into the blood capillaries. Fatty acids and glycerol are absorbed into the lacteals (lymph vessels). The distinction between capillary and lacteal is a regular 1-mark question.
writeBullets: Small intestine lined with villi — increase surface area for absorption
Glucose + amino acids → absorbed into blood capillaries
Fatty acids + glycerol → absorbed into lacteals (lymph vessels)
Villi features: thin wall (1 cell thick), large surface area, good blood supply

keyTerms.def: Tiny finger-like projections lining the small intestine — increase surface area for absorption
Lymph vessels inside villi that absorb fatty acids and glycerol
Movement of digested food molecules from the small intestine into the blood or lymph

keyPoints.heading: The large intestine — water recovery
keyPoints.content: By the time material reaches the large intestine, all useful nutrients have been absorbed. The large intestine absorbs water from the remaining material. What's left becomes faeces, stored in the rectum before passing out.
writeBullets: Large intestine absorbs water from remaining material
Remaining material becomes faeces, stored in the rectum

tables.title: Digestive organs — functions and secretions
rows: Carries food to stomach by peristalsis (wave-like muscle contractions)
Churns food; protein digestion; kills bacteria
Bile — emulsifies fats, neutralises stomach acid
Produces and releases digestive enzymes into small intestine
Final digestion; absorbs nutrients into blood and lymph
All three enzymes; site of absorption via villi

0.question: What is the correct definition of digestion?
options: Breaking food into smaller pieces by chewing
The breakdown of large, insoluble molecules into small, soluble ones
Producing enzymes to break down food
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right. 'Large, insoluble → small, soluble' is the phrase examiners want.
0.wrongFeedback: The key words are insoluble → soluble. Chewing is mechanical digestion, not digestion itself.

2.question: Why is bile NOT classified as an enzyme?
options: Because it is made in the liver, not the pancreas
Because it doesn't catalyse a chemical reaction — it physically emulsifies fats
Because it works on fats, not proteins or carbohydrates
Because it is stored in the gall bladder
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct. Enzymes catalyse chemical reactions. Bile physically breaks up fat droplets — no chemical reaction.
2.wrongFeedback: The reason is about what bile does, not where it's made. Bile emulsifies fats physically — no catalysis.

commonMistakes: Calling bile an enzyme — it isn't. It physically emulsifies fats rather than chemically digesting them.
Confusing the liver (makes bile) with the gall bladder (stores bile) — two different organs.
Saying fatty acids go into the blood — they go into the lacteals (lymph). Glucose and amino acids go into blood capillaries.
Forgetting the pancreas produces ALL THREE enzymes: amylase, protease AND lipase.
examTips: The definition of digestion must say 'large, insoluble' becoming 'small, soluble' — partial definitions lose marks.
For bile questions: say it is NOT an enzyme because it does not catalyse a reaction. Then say it emulsifies fats.
For absorption: always state the destination — capillary (blood) or lacteal (lymph) — not just 'absorbed'.
revisionCardBullets: Digestion = breakdown of large, insoluble molecules → small, soluble ones (absorbed into blood)
Route: mouth → oesophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → rectum
Mouth: amylase breaks down starch; mechanical digestion by teeth
Stomach: protease digests protein; HCl (pH 2) kills bacteria and creates optimal pH for protease
Liver: PRODUCES bile | Gall bladder: STORES bile (different organs!)
Bile = NOT an enzyme — emulsifies fats (physical, not chemical) → increases SA for lipase
Pancreas: produces amylase + protease + lipase (all three!)
Small intestine: final digestion + absorption via villi
Villi: large SA, one cell thick wall, good blood supply
Large intestine: absorbs water; forms faeces
